Louis Pasteur
Louis Pasteur

Contributions to:
-Chemistry
Stereochemistry
-Microbiology
Germ theory
Vaccination
Pasteurization
Pasteur’s Sketches (1848)
Before crystallization:
Solution is “optically inactive”
After crystallization:
Solutions of different crystals rotate light in opposite directions

Chiral object: an object that is not identical to its mirror image
Nonsuperimposable :
Symmetry can make objects identical to their mirror images
“Handedness” (chirality) around us

Jacobus “Henry” van 't Hoff Jr.
J. H. van ‘t Hoff
1852-1911
Contributions to chemistry:
-Tetrahedral carbon
-Stereochemistry
-Kinetics
-Thermodynamics
In 1874, van 't Hoff proposed the tetrahedral carbon atom as we know it in a Dutch publication and a small French book
This showed how there can be two equal, but opposite configurations of molecules
It also reconciled Pasteur and others’ observation of optical rotation in molecules
La chimie dans l'espace , 1874

Van ‘t Hoff took a position at the Veterinary College of Utrecht after completing his doctoral studies at the University of Utrecht in 1874
His theory was not well-received initially…
“Whoever thinks this worry seems exaggerated should read, if he is capable of it, the recent phantasmagorically frivolous puffery . . . on "The Arrangement of Atoms in Space." . . . A Dr. J. H. van't Hoff, of the Veterinary School of Utrecht, finds, it seems, no taste for exact chemical research. He has considered it more convenient to mount Pegasus (apparently on loan from the Veterinary School) and to proclaim in his "La chimie dans l'espace " how, during his bold flight to the top of the chemical Parnassus, the atoms appeared to him to be arranged in cosmic space." ~Hermann Kolbe (1877)
---------------------------------------------------------------------------
Stereogenic (chiral) center: an atom bonded to four different groups
Example: 1-bromo-1-chloroethane has a chiral center = 2 configurations are possible
Chirality in the tetrahedral carbon atom

Enantiomers – stereoisomers that are nonsuperimposable mirror images of each other
*describes a relationship between two structures*
Chiral molecule – molecule that has an enantiomer *describes a property of a molecule*
Achiral molecule – molecule that is the same as its mirror image
* molecules that have symmetry are usually achiral*
Terminology regarding chirality in molecules
Example: reaction of cyanide with an aldehyde
Example: reaction of cyanide with an aldehyde
Cyanide with acetone makes an achiral product
Symmetric molecules are a chiral
But wait, there’s more!

Label all of the stereogenic centers
Draw the other enantiomer of 2-bromobutane
Chirality in molecules

System for specifying which enantiomer is being discussed
1) Rank the groups in order of “priority”
2) Arrange chiral center with the lowest priority group pointing backwards .
If 1-2-3 is clockwise = ‘R’ configuration
if 1-2-3 is counterclockwise = ‘S’ configuration
R/S Nomenclature Rules for Stereogenic Centers

Rules for Assigning Priority
Molecular weight . Atoms with higher molecular weight have higher priority
Adjacent atoms . Look for the heaviest atom in the adjacent atoms – the group with most higher priority adjacent atom gets priority
Multiple bonds . If there are multiple bonds, treat that atom as if it is bound to the other atom based on the bond order (double bond = two of the other atoms, triple bond = three )
R/S Assignment Examples

Possibilities:
Lowest priority group backward:
Lowest priority group forward:
Lowest priority group in plane:
If you can’t see it…
Trick 1: rotate in space
Trick 2: swap two groups
(changes configuration)
R/S Assignment Tricks

Diastereomers: relationship between molecules that are stereoisomers but not mirror images of each other – just one chiral center is switched
Molecules with more than one stereogenic center

*a meso compound is achiral*

Enantiomers have identical properties: except 1) they rotate light in equal and opposite directions
2) they interact differently with other chiral molecules
Properties of enantiomers
Chiral molecules have a secret handshake

Resolution = separation of enantiomers
Ways of separating enantiomers: 1) resolution using diastereomeric salts
2) resolution using chiral chromatography
Resolution using diastereomeric salts
Resolution using chiral chromatography
